ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general okra food product entry; no specific entry for deep-fried spiced okra (Kurkuri Bhindi). Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers cooked okra; no specific code for deep-fried besan-coated okra. Approximate match used and documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Kurkuri Bhindi or deep-fried okra dish. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: battered with chickpea flour (besan) and rice flour

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Kurkuri Bhindi is approximately 150 g, representing a typical side dish serving at an Indian or fusion restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Okra (Bhindi), cooked; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for deep-fried coating adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kurkuri Bhindi is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked okra (bhindi) baseline combined with USDA FoodData Central data for chickpea flour (besan) and deep-frying fat absorption estimates. The input record's per_100g values (energy 300 kcal, protein 6.67 g, fat 23.33 g, carbohydrate 16.67 g) are consistent with this adapted composite and are retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: A Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — chickpea flour (besan) and okra analogous estimate; Atkinson FS et al., International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kurkuri Bhindi. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on: (1) okra is a low-GI vegetable with high soluble fibre (mucilage) that slows glucose absorption; (2) chickpea flour (besan) has a published GI of approximately 39 (University of Sydney GI Database); (3) rice flour coating is a minor component. The composite GI of approximately 40 is a defensible low-GI estimate. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 25.0) / 100 = 10.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100 g (450 mg per 150 g serving) is borderline for hypertension management; deep-frying adds significant fat load
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 80–100 g and request minimal added salt during preparation; the potassium content (~280 mg/100 g from okra) provides partial offset, but sodium remains a concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated GI (~40) and high dietary fibre (4.67 g/100 g) support glycaemic control, but deep-frying significantly increases caloric density and fat content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kurkuri Bhindi has a low estimated GI due to okra's soluble fibre and chickpea flour coating; limit portion to 80–100 g to manage total carbohydrate and caloric intake, and account for the besan coating in daily carbohydrate targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (23.33 g/100 g) from deep-frying and trans fat (0.33 g/100 g) are cardiovascular risk factors; saturated fat is relatively low at 3.33 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation significantly elevates total fat and introduces trans fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 80 g or request an air-fried or oven-baked preparation to reduce fat load and eliminate trans fat formation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that directly exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation; request air-fried or oven-baked Kurkuri Bhindi to retain the anti-inflammatory benefits of turmeric (curcumin) while eliminating the AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kurkuri Bhindi is a popular North Indian crispy fried okra dish in which fresh okra (bhindi / lady's finger) is slit lengthwise, coated in a spiced batter of chickpea flour (besan) and rice flour seasoned with turmeric, chilli powder, cumin, coriander, and salt, then deep-fried until golden and shatteringly crisp. The name 'kurkuri' means crispy in Hindi, and the dish delivers an intensely crunchy texture with a savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile. It is served as a side dish or snack alongside dal, roti, or rice in Indian cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 300 kcal, 6.67 g protein, 23.33 g fat (3.33 g saturated), 16.67 g carbohydrate, 4.67 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The high dietary fibre from okra's soluble mucilage supports glycaemic control and gut health. The estimated glycemic index is low (~40) due to the chickpea flour base and okra fibre. However, deep-frying substantially increases caloric density and introduces trans fat (0.33 g/100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free when prepared with certified gluten-free flours. In Singapore, it appears as a fusion side dish in Indian and Turkish-Indian restaurant menus.
common_user_queries: How many calories in crispy fried okra?
Is Kurkuri Bhindi good for diabetes?
What flour is used in Kurkuri Bhindi?
Is fried okra good for blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Kurkuri Bhindi is not listed as a discrete entry in any approved primary source (ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore). Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked okra baseline combined with USDA FoodData Central chickpea flour and deep-frying fat absorption data. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on chickpea flour GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): Kurkuri Bhindi is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; cholesterol is naturally zero and confirmed by the plant-based ingredient profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and micronutrient data for this specific deep-fried spiced okra preparation are not available as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN, USDA FoodData Central, or HPB Singapore database as of 2024. 3. potassium_mg set to 280.0 mg/100 g: estimated from ICMR-NIN okra data; not directly measured for this preparation. 4. The input record lists the dish under Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma cuisine on GrabFood; this is a menu categorisation artefact. Kurkuri Bhindi is an Indian dish and has been correctly classified as Indian cuisine. 5. Portions in the input record contained duplicates and inconsistencies; the canonical serving size of 150 g (1 plate) has been used. 6. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on chickpea flour GI (~39, University of Sydney GI Database) and okra soluble fibre contribution; this is an analogous estimate, not a direct measurement. 7. Trans fat at 0.33 g/100 g is retained from the input record as a plausible value for deep-fried preparations using commercial vegetable oils; this should be verified against the specific oil used.
